core.intro: Wherever you grow lychees in Australia the fundamentals are the same: a frost-free spot, deep and free-draining soil, a named air-layered or grafted variety, and a cool, dry winter to switch the tree from leaf to flower. The state sections cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere you can grow the tree.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Australian lychees are sold by named Chinese and Thai cultivars, and the choice matters more than with most fruit trees because some crop far more reliably than others. Kwai May Pink (also sold as Kwai Mai Pink, and historically as Bosworth No. 3) is the mainstay: small-seeded, very sweet, and one of the few that crops well almost every year from New South Wales to the far north. Wai Chee is a dependable late variety, Tai So is the old early commercial standard but can bear irregularly, and Bengal carries big, handsome fruit (around a large seed) in heavy crops, usually every second year. Salathiel is prized for eating quality and the most consistent fruit set of the lot. Erdon Lee is a large, late, high-quality fruit. Lychee shares its needs and many of its pests with its close relative the longan.

sections.heading: Seed size and fruit quality
sections.body: Lychee quality comes down largely to the seed. A full, fat seed leaves only a thin shell of flesh, while a small shrivelled seed (growers call it a chicken tongue seed) leaves the fruit almost solid aril. The best eating varieties are the small-seeded ones: Salathiel sets a chicken tongue seed in nearly every fruit, Kwai May Pink is reliably small-seeded, and a few types are even sold as seedless. Large-seeded varieties such as Bengal and Brewster still taste good but give you less to eat per fruit, so weigh seed size against how heavily and reliably the variety crops.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: A single lychee will fruit on its own. Each tree carries both functional male and functional female flowers, which open in succession on the same large panicle, so the tree is effectively self-fruitful and you do not need a second variety. What you do need is insects: lychee depends on bees to carry pollen between the flowers, so a hive or good wild bee activity, plus warm, still, dry days during the few weeks of flowering, does far more for your crop than planting a pollinator partner.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Plant in full sun in deep, free-draining, slightly acidic soil, in a frost-free position sheltered from strong wind (lychee wood is brittle and young trees resent exposure). The tree hates waterlogging and dislikes salty or strongly alkaline soils, so avoid low spots where water sits and improve heavy ground before planting. Mulch well to keep the shallow feeder roots cool and moist. Warm soil gets a new tree away fastest, so spring into early summer is the usual planting time (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Lychees are shallow-rooted and will not tolerate drying out, so keep the soil evenly moist under a good mulch. The danger window runs from flowering through to harvest, when water stress drops both yield and fruit size (one Australian trial saw a tree's gross return fall from about A$250 to A$35 under a severe drought), so this is the worst time to let a tree go thirsty, while at the other extreme the roots rot in waterlogged ground, so plant high and never let water sit. Feed little and often through the warm season and lean on potassium, which research links to better flowering: a worked Australian backyard program for a seven-year-old tree splits roughly 350 g of nitrogen, 170 g of phosphorus and 210 g of potassium across the year as urea plus muriate of potash, with a dressing after harvest. Keep nitrogen modest, since too much pushes leafy growth instead of flowers, and do not pile on potassium year after year. Lychee likes a slightly acidic soil (about pH 5.5 to 6.5), runs short of trace elements easily, and repays a little boron on the red volcanic soils of the eastern districts; ease right off feeding in autumn so the cool winter can switch the tree from leaf to flower.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Lychees ripen in clusters through the warmer months, broadly November to March depending on your region and variety. The catch is that lychee is not a fruit that ripens after picking: it will not get any sweeter off the tree, so pick only once the skin has fully coloured (most varieties turn pink to red) and a sample tastes sweet. The fruit is highly perishable, and the bright skin browns within a day or two of harvest even though the flesh underneath stays good, so refrigerate it, keep the humidity up, and eat it within a few days.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, air-layered (marcot) or grafted tree, never a seedling. Lychees do not come true from seed, seedlings can take ten years or more to bear and may never fruit well, while a marcot or grafted named variety usually starts cropping in about three to five years. Buy clean stock too: the lychee erinose mite spreads on infested nursery plants, so check the leaves for puckered, felty galls and start with mite-free trees. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties on sale right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Can I grow a lychee tree in a pot?
faqs.a: While it is young, yes. A lychee will live in a large pot for a few years, which lets you shelter it from frost and cold winters, but it is a big, long-lived tree that crops best in the ground in a warm climate. Treat a pot as a way to get started, or to keep a tree going in a cool district, rather than a permanent home for a cropping tree.

faqs.q: How long until a lychee tree fruits?
faqs.a: An air-layered (marcot) or grafted named variety usually starts bearing in about three to five years, with worthwhile crops from around year six. Seedlings can take ten years or more and the fruit is unpredictable, which is why named, propagated stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Why won't my lychee flower or fruit?
faqs.a: Lychees need a cool, dry winter to flower: several weeks of cool nights (below about 20 degrees) while the buds are just on the move. A mild or humid winter, a warm wet spring, or a young tree in soft leafy growth will all give you a tree full of leaves and no fruit.

faqs.q: Do lychees ripen after they are picked?
faqs.a: No. Lychees are not climacteric, so they do not sweeten or improve once picked. Pick them only when the skin has fully coloured and the fruit tastes sweet. The skin browns within a day or two, but the flesh keeps for several days if you store the fruit cool and humid.

QLD.intro: Growing lychees in Queensland. Queensland grows about nine in every ten of Australia's lychees, from the Atherton Tableland and the tropical coast down through the central and southern districts, so almost every named variety is at home somewhere in the state.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland has all the warmth a lychee wants, but the catch is winter. The tree needs a cool, dry spell, several weeks of nights below about 20 degrees with the buds just starting to move, to flower well, and it barely flowers at all once nights stay warmer than that. The drier, slightly cooler Tableland and the southern districts deliver that trigger more reliably than the warm, humid tropical coast, where getting a consistent crop is the hardest part of the job.

sections.heading: Where lychees grow in QLD
sections.body: About half the national crop comes from the north, around Cairns, Ingham and the Atherton Tableland. The rest comes from the central and southern districts, including Rockhampton, Bundaberg, Gympie, Nambour and the Sunshine Coast hinterland. Lychee farms run a long way down this coast, so your district, not just the state, decides which varieties and harvest timing suit you.

sections.heading: Which varieties suit QLD
sections.body: Early to mid-season varieties such as Fay Zee Siu, Souey Tung and Tai So do best in the warm north, while Kwai May Pink crops well right along the coast and into the south. The late varieties Wai Chee and Salathiel are better suited to the cooler southern districts, where the longer, milder lead-in lets them finish well.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland opens the national season. The earliest northern fruit (Souey Tung and Fay Zee Siu) can start around October and November, the main-season varieties run through December and January, and late Wai Chee can hang on the tree into February.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: The lychee erinose mite, which puckers leaves into felty galls, is established across Queensland's lychee districts and spreads on infested nursery plants, so always plant clean stock. Macadamia nutborer is the major fruit pest, and its damage is often blamed on fruit fly, which in fact rarely attacks lychees. Lorikeets and flying foxes will strip ripening fruit, so netting is the standard defence.

faqs.q: How big does a lychee tree get, and can I keep it smaller?
faqs.a: In the tropics a lychee can grow into a dense tree of 10 metres or more, but most backyard growers prune hard after harvest to hold it at a pickable 3 to 4 metres. Keeping it low also makes it far easier to throw a net over the canopy as the fruit colours up.

faqs.q: Why do my coastal Queensland lychees make leaves but no fruit?
faqs.a: Warm, humid coastal winters often fail to give the run of cool nights a lychee needs to flower, so the tree flushes leaves instead. Cooler, drier Tableland and southern sites flower more reliably, and choosing a variety known to crop in your district helps a lot.

WA.intro: Growing lychees in Western Australia. Lychee is a tropical tree, so in WA it belongs in the warm north: the Ord River around Kununurra and the irrigated Gascoyne around Carnarvon. Around Perth it is a gamble, because the mild winters often will not trigger reliable flowering.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: A lychee needs two things at once: a frost-free climate, and a cool, dry winter to flower, several weeks of nights below about 20 degrees while the buds are moving. WA's tropical north gets that distinct cool dry season; the Gascoyne is workable; but on the Swan Coastal Plain and through the Perth hills the winters are often too mild and too erratic to set flowers dependably, so a Perth lychee is best treated as a long-term, year-to-year gamble rather than a sure crop.

sections.heading: Where lychees grow in WA
sections.body: The Ord River Irrigation Area around Kununurra, in the East Kimberley, is WA's main tropical-fruit district and the part of the state best suited to lychee. The Gascoyne around Carnarvon is another major irrigated horticultural area that already grows a range of tropical and subtropical fruit. Perth and the South West can grow the tree, but fruit it only erratically.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: In the tropical north the fruit ripens through the warm season, broadly from around November onward. Perth trees, in the years when they do set, also ripen over summer, but crop too unreliably to count on a date.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's isolation and strict plant quarantine help keep out pests that are established in the eastern states, including the lychee erinose mite, a leaf-galling mite that travels on infested nursery plants. Even so, birds and, in the north, flying foxes will take ripening fruit, so net backyard trees as the crop colours up.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually certified and treated before entry, so many eastern nurseries will not post lychee trees here. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: Can you grow a lychee tree in Perth?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree, but fruiting is unreliable: Perth's mild winters often fail to deliver the run of cool nights a lychee needs to flower, so expect plenty of leafy growth and only the occasional crop. WA's dependable lychee country is the tropical north around Kununurra.

faqs.q: Which lychee varieties do best in WA's tropical north?
faqs.a: The same reliable, small-seeded varieties that succeed up the Queensland coast do well in the warm north: Kwai May Pink is the dependable all-rounder, and Salathiel is worth seeking out for eating quality. Start with clean, named, air-layered stock, and net the canopy as the fruit colours.

NSW.intro: Growing lychees in New South Wales. Lychee is a coastal, frost-free crop in NSW, confined to the warm far north of the state, where it ripens later than anywhere else in the country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Lychees need warmth and a frost-free site, so in NSW they are limited to the humid, subtropical far north coast. The colder inland and the tablelands are out: even a light frost will kill young trees, and the tree still needs a cool but frost-free winter to flower. Get the site right and northern NSW grows excellent fruit; get it wrong and the tree either freezes or never flowers.

sections.heading: Where lychees grow in NSW
sections.body: NSW grows only about a tenth of the national crop, all of it in the Northern Rivers and far north coast: districts such as Lismore, Alstonville and Ballina, and south to about Coffs Harbour. South of there the climate quickly becomes too cool and frost-prone for a dependable tree.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Northern NSW picks last in the country. Its cooler southern position pushes the season into the new year, with late varieties such as Wai Chee and Salathiel running from about January through to March, after Queensland has finished.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Northern NSW deals with the same pests as southern Queensland: macadamia nutborer in the fruit (its damage is often mistaken for fruit fly, which seldom attacks lychees), the leaf-galling lychee erinose mite, which makes buying clean stock important, and fruit-stealing birds and flying foxes, which netting keeps out.

faqs.q: How do I protect a young lychee from frost in northern NSW?
faqs.a: Young trees are the tender ones, so give a new lychee the warmest spot you have, ideally against a north-facing wall, and wrap it or cover it on frosty nights through its first winters. Once a tree is established and woody it shrugs off the odd light frost, but a hard frost can still set it back.

faqs.q: Can you grow a lychee south of the Northern Rivers?
faqs.a: It gets harder fast. Around the Sydney coast and cooler districts there is barely enough warmth for good fruiting and more frost risk for young trees, so try one only in the warmest, most sheltered corner or in a large pot you move under cover for winter, and treat any fruit as a bonus.

VIC.intro: Growing lychees in Victoria. Victoria is really too cold for lychees: the tree is frost-sensitive and the winters do not suit reliable fruiting, so in Victoria a lychee is a container or hothouse curiosity rather than a backyard crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: A lychee needs a frost-free climate and a warm growing season, and young trees are readily killed by frost. Victoria's cold, frosty winters fall outside what the tree can take, so an outdoor lychee in most of the state will struggle to survive, let alone fruit. Commercial lychee growing does not extend to Victoria for exactly this reason, and no district here offers a dependable backyard crop.

sections.heading: Growing one under cover
sections.body: If you want to try anyway, grow your lychee in a large pot you can move into a warm, bright, frost-free spot, a greenhouse, a sunroom, or hard against a sunny north-facing wall, over the cold months. Give it the warmest microclimate you have, protect it from frost every winter, and treat any fruit as a bonus rather than something to plan around. For a reliable crop in a cool climate, a hardier subtropical such as feijoa or loquat is a safer bet.

faqs.q: Can you grow a lychee tree in Victoria?
faqs.a: Only with effort, and rarely with fruit. Victoria's frosts and cool winters do not suit lychees, which are frost-sensitive subtropical trees. Your best chance is a potted tree kept frost-free and warm over winter, treated as an ornamental that might occasionally fruit.

faqs.q: Will a lychee survive a Melbourne winter outdoors?
faqs.a: A young tree is likely to be damaged or killed by frost, and even an established one will rarely get the warmth it needs to fruit. Grow it in a pot you can move under cover for winter, or choose a hardier subtropical fruit instead.
